splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate an employer's withdrawal liability from a multiemployer pension plan to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be selected before the statutory measurement date, providing employers with greater predictability about potential withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that plan actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current data even if it means assumptions are chosen after year-end.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts can apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, so the clock stops running and the court retains jurisdiction to revoke supervised release even after the nominal term would have expired.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release during absconding, and the term expires as originally set regardless of whether the supervisee fled.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) from raising any claim they previously presented in a successive § 2255 motion, in addition to the separate requirements in § 2255(h). If (b)(1) applies, it imposes a stricter gatekeeping requirement than Congress may have intended.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar in § 2244(b)(1) on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, in addition to § 2255(h)'s requirements.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners filing § 2255 motions, meaning they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The resolution affects the ability to challenge rules on mail-in voting, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices nationwide.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates for federal office suffer a cognizable competitive injury sufficient to establish Article III standing when challenging election rules that may affect the outcome of their race.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates do not have Article III standing to challenge election administration rules pre-election because the alleged injury is too speculative or generalized.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity trumps deference to agency interpretations in the criminal sentencing context. The Third Circuit held that agency deference trumps lenity, while the Fourth, Fifth, and Eleventh Circuits hold that lenity prevails. This split has deepened after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations of ambiguous criminal provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through delegated power.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference takes precedence over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous sentencing guidelines, deferring to the Commission's anti-defendant reading of ambiguous provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' means 'and' or 'or' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision. This determines whether defendants must meet all three criminal-history criteria to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether any single criterion is disqualifying, significantly affecting criminal sentencing outcomes.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or' in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference under Stinson and Kisor.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses is a valid interpretation of the guideline text, which does not mention inchoate offenses. Some circuits defer to the commentary; others hold the guideline text is unambiguous and excludes inchoate offenses.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of inchoate offenses is a reasonable interpretation of the ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling weight under Stinson/Kisor.

positions.label: Guideline text is unambiguous; commentary exceeds it
positions.summary: Holds that the definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline's scope rather than interpreting it.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state schedules a substance not controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally scheduled or whether any state-scheduled substance suffices. The Eighth Circuit holds there is no federal-law limitation, while other circuits require the substance to match federal schedules.

positions.label: No federal-law limitation — state scheduling suffices
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines even if the particular substance is not controlled under federal law, because the guideline text references 'state law' without limitation.

positions.label: Federal scheduling required
positions.summary: Holds that a prior state conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance at issue is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act, applying a categorical approach.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) can constitute or contribute to an 'extraordinary and compelling reason' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether non-retroactive sentencing changes can be considered as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar such consideration; others permit it as one factor. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment attempted to allow it, but courts disagree on whether the Commission exceeded its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law, whether alone or combined with other factors, may be considered as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot be extraordinary and compelling reasons
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in law, whether offered alone or in combination with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must use habeas corpus (28 U.S.C. §§ 2241-2255) rather than civil rights actions (e.g., Bivens) to challenge conditions of confinement that affect the fact or duration of imprisonment, such as BOP decisions regarding earned time credits and prerelease custody under the First Step Act.
splits.description: Circuits have a longstanding split on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for challenges to conditions or decisions that affect the duration of confinement, or whether prisoners may also bring civil rights actions. This split has evolved as new statutory schemes like the First Step Act create new categories of BOP discretionary decisions affecting release timing.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy for duration-of-confinement claims
positions.summary: Holds that when a prisoner challenges BOP actions affecting the fact or duration of confinement (such as denial of earned time credits), habeas corpus is the sole federal remedy and civil rights actions are unavailable.

positions.label: Civil rights actions also available for certain confinement challenges
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners are not categorically limited to habeas corpus and may bring civil rights actions to challenge certain BOP decisions affecting confinement, particularly when the conditions challenged are not directly about release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may impose sentences at supervised release revocation that are expressly based on punishing the defendant for the violation, rather than being limited to the purposes of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether punishment is a permissible purpose of a sentence imposed upon revocation of supervised release. The Sixth Circuit allows district courts expressly to punish defendants for violations, while other circuits hold that revocation sentences may only serve the limited purposes of deterrence and protecting the public, not punishment for the violation itself.

positions.label: Punishment is permissible at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for supervised release violations, placing the Sixth Circuit at the extreme of the circuit split on the permissible purposes of revocation sentences.

positions.label: Punishment is not a permissible purpose at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that sentences imposed upon revocation of supervised release should be based on the defendant's breach of trust and the need to protect the public, not on punishing the underlying violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who in good faith convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit holds it belongs to the estate; other circuits have held it belongs to the debtor, creating a split on the interpretation of § 348(f)(1)(A).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the bankruptcy estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), combined with § 541(a)'s broad definition of estate property, means post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtor, consistent with the Bankruptcy Code's structure and § 348(f)'s legislative history designed to protect good-faith converting debtors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an order denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (triggering the civil appeal deadline) or part of the criminal case itself. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, but there is disagreement among the circuits.

positions.label: Civil appeal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the longer civil notice-of-appeal deadline applies rather than the shorter criminal deadline.

positions.label: Criminal appeal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the underlying criminal case, so the shorter criminal notice-of-appeal deadline governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal medical records in litigation is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as an order implicating a 'value of a high order.'
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether denials of motions to seal medical information are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine. The D.C. Circuit held that medical privacy is a value of 'high order' warranting immediate review, while other circuits have taken different approaches to the collateral order doctrine's application to sealing orders.

positions.label: Immediately appealable under collateral order doctrine
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying a motion to seal medical records is immediately appealable because maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' that would be effectively unreviewable after final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine because the privacy interest can be adequately vindicated through other means, such as protective orders or appeal after final judgment.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 or related civil rights statutes?
splits.description: The Second Circuit's approach to legal questions governing discrimination in hospitality and business establishments was found sufficiently unsettled to warrant Supreme Court review, suggesting a circuit split on the applicable standards for liability and damages in such cases.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: The Second Circuit applied standards for discrimination claims in hospitality settings that prompted Supreme Court review, suggesting its approach diverges from other circuits.

positions.label: Other circuits' approach
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different standards for evaluating discrimination claims in business establishments, creating a split that SCOTUS granted cert to resolve.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier... with respect to the transportation of property,' extends to preempt state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligently selecting or supervising motor carriers. This affects liability exposure across the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are 'related to' the broker's services with respect to the transportation of property and are therefore preempted by the FAAAA.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring or supervision of carriers are not preempted because they do not relate to the broker's 'prices, routes, or services.'

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas corpus grounds — such as alleged errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence — may be considered in compassionate release motions. Some circuits allow such considerations as one factor, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for § 2255 habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider claimed legal errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot use compassionate release for habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for relitigating the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be brought under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
